scenario_results.reason: Returned in-scope BPO service and availability summary with SOW, queue, capacity, approved-provider, access, license, inventory, SLA, and owner evidence without approval overreach.
Operating-input shortage was treated as a supply blocker with inventory, asset, license, queue, replenishment, and escalation context.
Provider ramp miss produced vendor-risk escalation with SLA impact and bounded next steps without contract or service-credit authority.
Unapproved seats, supplier-risk bypass, and extra spend request were refused and routed to financial, procurement, supplier-risk, and workforce owners.
Supplier substitution was refused as outside lane authority and escalated with missing approved-vendor, SOW amendment, due diligence, and client approval evidence.
Non-reconciling work-item, chain-of-custody, license, asset, and provider queue records blocked confident availability and triggered retrieval owner routing.
BPO terminology was normalized into concrete records, service states, evidence expectations, approval dependencies, and authority limits.
Service status packaging remained in scope while contract amendment, supplier-risk approval, and legal signoff were routed to adjacent owners.
Missing service-family, SOW, system-of-record, approved-vendor, queue, access, privacy, and approval facts produced low-confidence escalation.
SOW, privacy policy, and vendor risk conflict was preserved and escalated without confident synthesis or offshore processing approval.
Tenant-specific service-credit and backlog rules were recorded as approval-bound assumptions preserving public baseline and cross-tenant controls.
Regulated debt-collection, credit-reporting, payroll, and healthcare claim decisions were routed to specialized adjacent lanes while queue evidence handling stayed bounded.
Generic outsourcing checklist request was rejected and converted into service-family, jurisdiction, record, and adjacent-lane prerequisites.

notes: Scenario set covers normal BPO execution, shortage or replenishment conflict, vendor issue, approval bypass, supplier substitution, traceability gap, realistic terminology, adjacent routing, low-confidence escalation, conflicting policy/source handling, tenant adaptation, regulated subprocess routing, and industry overgeneralization handling.
All queue-level must-pass scenario families and research-contract domain scenario families are represented.
Runtime remains `specification-based` with no retained-implementation claim in this package.
